summary: Epic Games deliberately violated App Store rules by adding direct payment to Fortnite on iOS, offering users a 20% discount by bypassing Apple's 30% cut. Apple responded by removing Fortnite from the App Store, after which Epic immediately filed a prepared lawsuit and released a parody of Apple's iconic '1984' ad. Gruber notes that Epic orchestrated the entire sequence — the violation, the lawsuit, and the ad campaign — as a coordinated attack on Apple's App Store model. He observes that Epic is fighting as much in the court of public opinion as in actual court, and that Apple stands to lose more in brand equity than in revenue regardless of the outcome.
key_points: Epic's direct payment feature was shipped via an in-app update specifically to circumvent App Store review, and Epic knew exactly how Apple would respond
Apple chose the 'medium difficulty' response of removing Fortnite from the App Store rather than the lighter option of blocking updates or the nuclear option of disabling installed copies
Epic's 20% discount doesn't fully pass savings to users — Epic actually nets more per transaction through direct payment than through Apple's system
Epic's legal filing asks for the whole shebang: making iOS as open to third-party software and app stores as the Mac
The campaign, including the '1984' parody ad, is directed at an audience already hostile to Apple, giving it a preaching-to-the-choir quality
money_quotes: Epic shipped this feature via an update that was delivered entirely through the Fortnite app itself, not through a new version of the app submitted through the App Store.
The only question was what Apple would do.
It was very clear this morning that Epic knew exactly what it was doing, and how Apple was likely to respond.
There's a preaching-to-the-choir aspect to Epic's entire campaign, though.
The ad is in some ways a deft parody, and it is very saucy, but one thing it is not is funny.
Epic, in a very savvy way, is waging this war as much or more in the court of public opinion as they are in any court of law.
Apple stands to lose more in brand equity than in dollars, no matter how this turns out.
What makes this a fair fight is that Fortnite is already installed on so many millions of iOS devices. Popularity is power.
key_insight: Epic's coordinated legal and PR assault on Apple's App Store was designed to win in the court of public opinion as much as in any courtroom, and Apple's real vulnerability is brand equity, not revenue.
